elements.sent-2411-001: We present a novel technique using Fourier series and Laguerre polynomials to represent morphological features of disc galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-002: To demonstrate the utility of this technique, we study the evolution of asymmetry in a sample of disc galaxies drawn from the Extended Groth Strip and imaged by the JWST Cosmic Evolution Early Release Science Survey as well as archival HST observations.
elements.sent-2411-003: We measure disc asymmetry as the amplitude of the of the $m = 1$ Fourier harmonic for galaxies within redshift ranges of $1 < z < 4$.
elements.sent-2411-004: We show that when viewed in shorter rest frame wavelengths, disc galaxies have a higher asymmetry as the flux is dominated by star forming regions.
elements.sent-2411-005: We find generally low asymmetry at rest frame infrared wavelengths, where our metric tracks asymmetry in morphological features such as bars and spiral arms.
elements.sent-2411-006: We show that higher mass galaxies have lower asymmetry and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-007: Higher asymmetry in lower mass galaxies comes from lower mass galaxies (typically) having higher star formation rates.
elements.sent-2411-008: We measure the relation between disc galaxy asymmetry and redshift and find no conclusive relationship between them.
elements.sent-2411-009: We demonstrate the utility of the Fourier-Laguerre technique for recovering physically informative asymmetry measurements as compared to rotational asymmetry measurements.
elements.sent-2411-010: We also release the software pipeline and quantitative analysis for each galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-011: Since the first `spiral nebulae' galaxies were discovered in the 17th century, much research has been dedicated to understanding their morphology and evolution.
elements.sent-2411-012: Morphological classification systems like the Hubble Sequence \citep{Hubble.1926 have stood the test of time as a straightforward proxy to describe galaxy evolution \citep{Buta_etal(2011),Buta_etal(2015) .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Our understanding of such classifications systems have greatly improved due to the rapid advancement of technology.
elements.sent-2411-014: The deep sky surveys by Hubble Space Telescope (HST) were revolutionary as they provided an opportunity to peer into realms never observed before \citep{Buta_etal(2011) .}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, high redshift galaxies in these surveys often cannot be resolved to the level required for detailed classification and appear to have an irregular or peculiar shape leading to a `classification crisis'\citep{vandenBergh(1996),Conselice_(2003) .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Furthermore, owing to the filters available, HST can only investigate galactic structures in the optical wavelength out until $z\simeq 2.8$ beyond which only the UV rest frame wavelength can be probed \citep{Ferreira_etal(2022),Jacob_etal(2023) .}
elements.sent-2411-017: These shorter wavelengths are dominated by young forming stars \citep{Bruzual_Charlot(2003),Jacob_etal(2023) , which can bias morphological classification.}
elements.sent-2411-018: With the commissioning of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) a new era in astronomy dawned.
elements.sent-2411-019: Since becoming operational, JWST has provided an opportunity to reconsider our understanding of galaxy evolution and the physical processes that govern it by leveraging its higher angular resolution and longer wavelength imaging capabilities \citep{Ferreira_etal(2022) .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The advent of novel technology like JWST has also led to an increase in the amount of data at high redshift allowing astronomers to better classify galaxies in the early Universe.
elements.sent-2411-021: While the traditional methods like visual classification by individual science teams \citep[e.g.][]{Ferreira.JWST.2023, Kartaltepe_etal(2023), Jacob_etal(2023) still prevail, newer projects like Galaxy Zoo have emerged that rely on the public to classify images from datasets like the Sloan Digital Sky Survey \citep[SDSS;][]{Lintott_etal2011,Smith_etal(2024) .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: To further improve efficiency, artificial intelligence and machine learning models are also being trained on the classifications made through different citizen science projects \citep[e.g.][]{Gordon.etal(2024) .}
elements.sent-2411-023: This paper, inspired by the work characterising detailed structure in simulations \citep{Weinberg.etal.2020,Johnson.etal.2023 , presents a novel classification system using a combination of Fourier and Laguerre polynomials.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The combination is an effective modelling framework as they compresses large pixel datasets to a few physically meaningful coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-025: The coefficients are a description of a galactic surface brightness distribution.
elements.sent-2411-026: While visual classification becomes more ambiguous when peering into higher redshifts due to decreasing resolution, the Fourier-Laguerre method performs efficiently and reproducibly as it relies directly on quantifiable pixel information.
elements.sent-2411-027: This method can be considered as a form of unsupervised learning since it does not require prior training data \citep{Johnson.etal.2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Several studies have already looked into using Fourier series to describe morphological features such as bars, spiral arms and asymmetry \citep[e.g.][]{Reichard(2009),Ghosh.etal.2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Our model goes a step further by including Laguerre polynomials to represent the exponential surface mass density profile of disc galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-030: Early-Universe disc galaxies are crucial to characterise as they provide the key to understanding the dynamical state of the early Universe.
elements.sent-2411-031: While at high redshift the hierarchical construction of galaxies could destroy the fragile discs, observations suggests that many discs exist in the early Universe \citep{Robertson_etal(2023) .}
elements.sent-2411-032: To improve our understanding of disc structures, the focus of this paper is the evolution of disc asymmetry.
elements.sent-2411-033: In the past few decades, observational studies suggest that disc asymmetry (measured using $m = 1$ Fourier mode) show a positive correlation with starburst occurrences \citep[e.g.][]{Zaritsky.Rix.1997 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Using a similar method, \citet{Reichard(2009) also found a strong relation between asymmetry in disc galaxies and the youth of the stellar population present in SDSS galaxies.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Following this, \cite{Zaritsky.etal.2013 measured the $m = 1$ related lopsidedness in a local volume-complete sample from the Spitzer Survey of Stellar Structure in Galaxies (S$^4$G) survey, and concluded that the presence of structure in the dark matter halo could be sufficient to induce observed asymmetries.}
elements.sent-2411-036: More recently, \citet{Yesuf.2021 found that disc asymmetries correlated with higher specific star formation rate at a given stellar mass.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In this work, we build on these earlier analyses and introduce a novel Fourier-Laguerre expansion framework for image analysis across multiple bands.
elements.sent-2411-038: The paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-039: Section~\ref{sec: Methodology provides context for our model and explains the working of our pipeline.}
elements.sent-2411-040: In Section~\ref{sec: Dataset we detail the datasets and databases referred to when identifying disc galaxies to expand through the pipeline.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Section~\ref{sec: Results presents the main results of the paper, focusing on characterizing the asymmetry of disc galaxies.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The paper concludes with a synopsis of our main results and future work.
elements.sent-2411-044: We introduce a new pipeline for Fourier-Laguerre EXpansions: FLEX.
elements.sent-2411-045: Compared to traditional morphological classifications, FLEX retains more galaxy morphology information.
elements.sent-2411-046: We use FLEX to measure the asymmetry of galaxies by calculating the Fourier-Laguerre coefficients that best represent a given galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-047: In this section we briefly describe the technique.
elements.sent-2411-048: Further details are located in Appendices: we explain the user experience in Appendix~\ref{app: A .}
elements.sent-2411-049: The mathematical details and related uncertainties of the Fourier-Laguerre expansions are presented in Appendix~\ref{app: B .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Our treatment of centring and scale length optimisation is described in Appendix~\ref{app: C .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Fourier series are the natural basis expansion for describing azimuthal symmetry present in galactic structure.
elements.sent-2411-052: The azimuthal structure of discs exhibits low order multiplicity (denoted $m$), such that Fourier expansions converge quickly (i.e. with fewer than ten harmonics).
elements.sent-2411-053: Previous studies ranging from early automated classification systems \citep[e.g.][]{Odewahn.2002 to more recent efforts \citep[e.g.][]{Ghosh.etal.2024 have mainly focused on a Fourier approach in successive radial annuli to describe galaxy structure and characterise morphological features in disc galaxies such as bars and spirals.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: In FLEX, the user can choose an $m_{\rm max}$ for the Fourier expansion order, setting the maximum multiplicity to be analysed.
elements.sent-2411-055: We choose $m_{\rm max =1$ in this paper, as explained below, and defer additional multiplicity investigations to future works (cf.}
elements.sent-2411-057: In the radial dimension, disc galaxies largely follow an exponential surface brightness profile \citep{Freeman(1970) , with many also featuring a super-exponential compact bulge in parametric decompositions \citep[e.g.][]{Laurikainen_etal(2016) .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Our expansion technique uses Laguerre polynomials as the radial basis functions.
elements.sent-2411-059: The exponential weighting function of Laguerre polynomials closely approximates the expected exponential profile of a typical disc galaxy, making Laguerre polynomials the ideal basis functions for expanding disc galaxies (refer to Appendix~\ref{app: B for more detail).}
elements.sent-2411-060: In FLEX, the user chooses a maximum Laguerre polynomial order, $n_{\rm max}$.
elements.sent-2411-061: Higher values of $n_{\rm max}$ result in finer structure resolution; however, the coefficients become increasingly uncertain owing to the spatial resolution of images.
elements.sent-2411-062: We return to this point when discussing uncertainties in Appendix~\ref{app: B .}
elements.sent-2411-063: By combining Fourier and Laguerre functions to make a two-dimensional basis, we can efficiently model the surface brightness distribution of discs in fewer terms than a corresponding Fourier-annulus expansion, with only physically motivated assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-064: The combination of polynomials enables compression of large pixel datasets to a few {\it physically meaningful Fourier-Laguerre coefficients that can be used to study features like asymmetries, bars, spiral arms, and rings.}
elements.sent-2411-065: We restrict our analysis in this work to asymmetries, which are the $m=1$ set of coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-066: Furthermore, these coefficients are also unaffected by the inclination of the galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-067: As shown by several past studies \citep[e.g.][]{Reichard(2009),Amvrosiadis_etal.(2024) $m = 1$ has widely been used to track the asymmetry of a galaxy's surface density.}
elements.sent-2411-068: We further distil the radial information in the coefficients into a single metric measuring the strength of asymmetry in a galaxy, $A_1$ (see Appendix~\ref{app: B for details).}
elements.sent-2411-069: The $A_{1}$ metric quantifies the asymmetry of a galaxy's light distribution.

elements.subsection-2411-004: Asymmetry as a Dynamical Classification Tool
elements.sent-2411-151: Mergers are widely known to affect galaxy asymmetry and are a consequence of the hierarchical formation of structure predicted by $\Lambda$-cold dark matter model \citep[e.g.][]{Bottrell.2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-152: The impact of mergers on SFR has widely been researched and observations show that mergers can trigger star formation \citep{Pearson_etal(2019) , which would appear in the shorter wavelength regime.}
elements.sent-2411-153: In addition to possibly forming new stars, mergers can distort the shapes of galaxies and build their stellar masses.
elements.sent-2411-154: Stars in galaxies can be traced back to either of two origins: {\it in situ (stars formed from gas within the galaxy it belongs to) or {\it ex situ (stars formed outside of the host galaxy in a system that has since merged, \citealt{VRodriguez-Gomez_etal(2016) ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Hydrodynamic cosmological simulations such as the Illustris Project \citep{Vogelsberger(2014), Genel_etal(2014) have been extensively used to study the role of {\it in situ and {\it ex situ stars in galaxy evolution.}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: Studies using this suite of simulations \citep{Lackner_etal.(2012),VRodriguez-Gomez_etal(2016),Pillepich_etal(2018) suggest that {\it ex situ star formation prevails largely in galaxies with stellar mass $ > 10^{11 {\rm M _\odot$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-158: These observations are also dependent on redshift with lower redshift galaxies having higher {\it ex situ star formation fractions.}

elements.sent-2411-225: In this work, we characterise the asymmetry of 271 disc galaxies lying within the EGS field imaged by JWST in CEERS and archival HST observations.
elements.sent-2411-226: For this work, we expand previously identified disc galaxies \citep{Ferreira.JWST.2023 within redshift ranges of $1~<~z~<~4$ using the FLEX pipeline.}
elements.sent-2411-227: This novel framework uses Fourier-Laguerre polynomials to compress pixel information into coefficients that describe galaxy morphology.
elements.sent-2411-228: Compared to traditional nonparametric classification methods, FLEX retains information on both the exponential profile of a disc galaxy as well as physically informative deviations.
elements.sent-2411-229: In this work, we use FLEX to measure the asymmetry of a galaxy using the set of $m=1$ radial coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-230: The $m=1$ radial coefficients describe the asymmetry (lopsidedness) of galaxy light distributions.
elements.sent-2411-231: The main conclusions of our work are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-232: Asymmetry is higher at shorter rest frame wavelengths (upper panel of Figure~\ref{fig:main_plot_2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-233: This is because in bluer filters, clumps of young stellar population dominate the emission (bottom left panel of Figure~\ref{fig:main_plot_1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-234: Redder filters can be used to probe asymmetry of features like bars and can be used to determine the underlying structural asymmetry of disc galaxies (bottom right panel of Figure~\ref{fig:main_plot_1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-235: Asymmetry does not evolve with redshift in the UV rest frame region (bottom panel of Figure~\ref{fig:main_plot_2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-236: However, a larger mass limited sample is needed to find a conclusive relation between asymmetry and redshift.
elements.sent-2411-237: Disc galaxies with lower stellar mass are more asymmetric and vice versa in the F444W filter (Figure~\ref{fig:main_plot_3 ).}
elements.sent-2411-238: This suggests that the structural asymmetry, determined at longer wavelengths, is inversely proportional to the stellar mass of a disc galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-239: This aligns with earlier analyses showing that lower stellar mass galaxies have higher presence of star formation rate \citep{Cole_etal(2023), Simmonds_etal(2024) .}
elements.sent-2411-240: We also conclude that for a given $A_{1}$ value, higher stellar mass galaxies appear to be located at high redshifts.
elements.sent-2411-241: The asymmetry of a disc galaxy is positively correlated with sSFR in the UV rest frame region (Figure~\ref{fig:main_plot_4 ).}

elements.sent-2411-252: Pipeline framework FLEX is an automated pipeline which can accept a list of user input EGS galaxy IDs and returns postage stamps, expansion coefficients, and asymmetry measurements.
elements.sent-2411-253: The process begins with locating the desired galaxy within the provided FITS images using reported (RA,~Dec) values from the CANDELS catalogue \citep{Stefanon_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-254: If a galaxy cannot be located, it is reported to the user and saved to a `unclassifiable' log file.
elements.sent-2411-255: Once located, a 110 by 110 pixel postage stamp of the galaxy is created centred around the given (RA,~Dec).
elements.sent-2411-256: To reduce interference, bright pixels outside the desired galaxy's radius are masked using sigma clipping.
elements.sent-2411-257: Refer to Section~\ref{sec: Dataset for detailed explanation about masking.}
elements.sent-2411-258: A first estimate of the scale radius of the galaxy is determined using scipy.optimize.curve\_fit to fit an exponential profile for the surface brightness profile of the galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-259: Following this estimate, we check that the galaxy image is not at the edge of the FITS file or empty.
elements.sent-2411-260: If it meets either of the conditions, the galaxy details are appended to the same `unclassifiable' log file.
elements.sent-2411-261: Once all filter image details are saved onto an HDF5 formatted file, the centre and scale length for the expansion (Appendix~\ref{app: C ) are determined.}
elements.sent-2411-262: These values are then set as parameters in the Fourier-Laguerre coefficients calculations.
elements.sent-2411-263: The determined Fourier-Laguerre coefficients are saved onto the same HDF5 file as the filter images upon completion.
elements.sent-2411-264: To visualise the expanded surface density, the pipeline displays a contour map of the expanded surface density (Equation~\ref{eq:5 ) alongside an amplitude chart for the filter the galaxy was expanded in.}

elements.sent-2411-298: We treat four sources of uncertainty in the calculation of $A_1$: (1) formal image uncertainty; (2) expansion truncations; (3) centre related uncertainty; (4) masking related uncertainty.

elements.sent-2411-303: The $n_{\rm max}$ value after which $A_1$ shows least variance informs the choice of the order that is required for each galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-304: In the convergence regime, the standard deviation of $A_1$ about the mean is recorded as the expansion truncation uncertainty.
